name: Predictive ML Model Pipeline
description: Machine learning pipeline that ingests historical and real-time data, engineers features, trains/serves predictive models, and delivers forecasts to business systems. Covers the full MLOps lifecycle from data collection through model serving and monitoring.
logisticsContext: Predictive models power freight rate forecasting, demand prediction, ETA estimation, fraud detection, and capacity planning. Model accuracy directly impacts revenue (pricing), operations (capacity), and risk management (fraud).

nodes.label: Data Sources
nodes.description: External and internal data feeds that supply raw inputs to the ML pipeline. Includes shipment history databases, market rate feeds (DAT, Freightos), carrier performance APIs, weather services, port congestion feeds, fuel price indices, and economic indicators. Data quality and integrity at this layer directly determines model accuracy downstream.
vulnerabilities: Data poisoning through manipulated market feeds or falsified carrier performance metrics designed to skew model predictions
Supply chain attacks on third-party API providers injecting corrupted data into training pipelines
Stale or delayed data feeds causing models to train on outdated market conditions without detection
Adversarial manipulation of publicly available data sources (fuel indices, port statistics) to influence model outputs

nodes.label: Data Ingestion Layer
nodes.description: ETL/ELT pipelines and streaming ingestion services that collect, validate, transform, and load raw data into the data warehouse and feature store. Handles batch imports (nightly shipment syncs), real-time streams (market rate ticks, GPS pings), schema validation, deduplication, and data quality checks. Typically built on Apache Kafka, Spark, or Airflow.
vulnerabilities: Schema injection attacks where malformed records bypass validation and corrupt downstream feature computation
Pipeline configuration tampering altering transformation logic to silently bias training data
Insufficient input validation allowing SQL injection or command injection through data field values
Backfill vulnerabilities where historical data re-ingestion overwrites corrected records with poisoned originals

nodes.label: Data Warehouse
nodes.description: Central repository for historical and curated datasets used in model training, validation, and backtesting. Stores cleaned shipment records, historical rates, carrier performance logs, demand signals, and ground truth labels. Serves as the single source of truth for all ML experimentation and production training jobs.
vulnerabilities: Training data poisoning through insertion of crafted records that introduce systematic bias into model predictions
Label corruption where ground truth values are modified to degrade model accuracy on specific prediction targets
Unauthorized access to sensitive pricing history or customer-specific rate data used in training sets
Data lineage gaps making it impossible to trace which records influenced a specific model version

nodes.label: Feature Store
nodes.description: Centralized repository of computed, versioned features used for both model training and real-time inference. Stores pre-computed aggregations (carrier on-time rates, lane averages, seasonal indices), real-time features (current fuel price, weather score), and feature metadata (data types, freshness SLAs, lineage). Ensures training-serving consistency by providing the same feature computation logic for both contexts.
vulnerabilities: Feature skew between training and serving causing silent model degradation when production features diverge from training-time computation
Feature poisoning through manipulation of upstream aggregation logic to systematically bias specific feature values
Unauthorized feature access exposing proprietary business logic embedded in feature engineering (e.g., competitive pricing signals)
Stale feature values served during real-time inference due to cache invalidation failures or pipeline delays

nodes.label: Training Pipeline
nodes.description: Orchestrates end-to-end model training: dataset sampling, feature selection, hyperparameter tuning, cross-validation, model fitting, and evaluation against holdout sets. Runs on GPU/CPU clusters (SageMaker, Vertex AI, Kubeflow). Produces trained model artifacts, evaluation metrics, and training metadata for the experiment tracker and model registry.
vulnerabilities: Training pipeline compromise injecting backdoors into model weights that activate on specific input patterns (trojan attacks)
Hyperparameter manipulation degrading model performance on targeted prediction classes while maintaining overall accuracy
Dependency supply chain attacks through compromised ML libraries (scikit-learn, PyTorch, XGBoost) used in the training stack
Resource exhaustion attacks triggering excessive GPU/compute consumption through crafted training configurations

nodes.label: ML Experiment Tracker
nodes.description: Records and compares all training runs with their parameters, metrics, datasets, and artifacts. Provides reproducibility, audit trails, and model lineage tracking. Typically MLflow, Weights & Biases, or Neptune. Enables A/B test analysis and model comparison for promotion decisions.
vulnerabilities: Experiment metadata tampering to falsify model performance metrics and promote underperforming or backdoored models
Insufficient access controls allowing unauthorized users to modify experiment results or delete audit trails
Artifact substitution swapping legitimate model binaries with compromised versions in the experiment store
Log injection attacks embedding malicious payloads in experiment metadata displayed in tracking dashboards

nodes.label: Model Registry
nodes.description: Version-controlled repository of trained model artifacts with lifecycle management. Stores serialized models, serving configurations, A/B test allocations, approval workflows, and rollback history. Gates model promotion from staging to production through validation checks and human approval. Central point of control for what models are actively serving predictions.
vulnerabilities: Model supply chain attacks replacing registered model artifacts with trojaned versions that produce adversary-controlled predictions
Insufficient approval workflows allowing untested or malicious models to be promoted directly to production
Version rollback exploitation reverting to a known-vulnerable model version to re-enable a previously patched attack vector
Metadata poisoning altering model tags or descriptions to mislead operators about model capabilities or training data scope

nodes.label: Model Serving
nodes.description: Inference infrastructure that loads trained models from the registry and executes predictions in real-time or batch mode. Handles feature retrieval, model execution, prediction post-processing, and response formatting. Runs on dedicated serving infrastructure (TensorFlow Serving, TorchServe, Triton, SageMaker Endpoints) with auto-scaling, canary deployments, and shadow mode testing.
vulnerabilities: Adversarial input attacks crafting feature vectors designed to force specific prediction outputs (e.g., artificially low fraud scores)
Model extraction through systematic querying to reverse-engineer proprietary pricing or scoring algorithms
Side-channel attacks inferring training data membership or sensitive feature values from prediction confidence patterns
Resource exhaustion through high-volume prediction requests designed to degrade serving latency or trigger auto-scaling costs

nodes.label: Prediction API
nodes.description: REST/gRPC endpoint that exposes model predictions to downstream business systems. Handles authentication, rate limiting, request validation, response formatting, and SLA enforcement. Translates raw model outputs into business-meaningful predictions (dollar amounts, probability scores, time estimates). Acts as the trust boundary between the ML platform and consuming applications.
vulnerabilities: Input manipulation sending crafted API payloads designed to produce adversary-favorable predictions (e.g., suppressed fraud alerts)
Rate limit bypass through distributed requests allowing bulk model extraction or prediction harvesting
API key leakage exposing prediction endpoints to unauthorized consumers who can exploit model outputs
Insufficient input validation allowing injection of out-of-distribution feature values that produce unreliable predictions

nodes.label: Business Applications
nodes.description: Downstream systems that consume model predictions and present them to end users or automated workflows. Includes pricing UIs (rate quoting tools), operations dashboards (capacity planning screens), booking systems (automated rate proposals), fraud review queues, and ETA tracking interfaces. These systems act on predictions and are the ultimate consumers of model output.
vulnerabilities: Prediction misuse where business logic over-relies on model outputs without human review for high-stakes decisions
Display manipulation presenting predictions without confidence intervals, leading users to treat uncertain forecasts as definitive
Feedback loop gaming where users systematically override predictions to bias future training data in their favor
Cross-system data leakage exposing proprietary pricing predictions to unauthorized users through shared dashboards

nodes.label: Model Monitor
nodes.description: Continuous monitoring system that tracks model performance, data drift, prediction distribution shifts, and operational health in production. Detects concept drift (changing relationship between features and outcomes), data drift (shifting input distributions), and model degradation. Triggers alerts and automated retraining workflows when performance drops below thresholds.
vulnerabilities: Alert suppression attacks manipulating monitoring thresholds or metrics to hide model degradation caused by adversarial inputs
Monitoring evasion through gradual, low-magnitude data poisoning that stays below drift detection thresholds
False positive flooding generating excessive alerts to cause alert fatigue and mask genuine model compromise
Metric manipulation tampering with performance baselines to make a degraded model appear healthy

nodes.label: Ground Truth Feedback
nodes.description: Collects actual outcomes (real delivery times, actual market rates, confirmed fraud cases) and compares them against model predictions to compute accuracy metrics and generate labeled training data for model retraining. Feedback latency varies from minutes (fraud confirmation) to weeks (final shipment settlement). Critical for continuous model improvement and drift correction.
vulnerabilities: Feedback poisoning injecting false ground truth labels to systematically degrade model accuracy during retraining
Delayed feedback exploitation where adversaries act during the gap between prediction and outcome confirmation
Selective feedback bias where certain outcome categories are underreported, skewing retraining data distribution
Label flipping attacks modifying confirmed outcomes (e.g., marking fraudulent transactions as legitimate) to corrupt retraining sets

edges.label: Promoted model artifacts and serving config
Model health status and rollback signals
